title: Psalms 29
subtitle: The Voice of the LORD over the Waters

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “The Voice of the LORD Is Powerful”

heb.paragraph: "The voice (qōl) of the LORD is over the waters" (v.3). Seven thunders of the LORD’s voice: over waters (v.3), powerful (v.4), majestic (v.4), breaks cedars (v.5), makes Lebanon skip (v.6). The storm psalm: God’s voice IS the thunder. Each "voice of the LORD" is a thunderclap.

panels.ctx: A hymn to God’s power in the storm. The heavenly beings are called to worship (vv.1–2). Then seven "voice of the LORD" declarations trace the storm from sea to land: over waters (v.3), powerful and majestic (v.4), shattering cedars and making mountains skip (vv.5–6).

cross.note: "Listen! Listen to the roar of his voice, to the rumbling that comes from his mouth" — the same thunder theology.
"From the throne came flashes of lightning, rumblings and peals of thunder" — the heavenly storm.

notes.note: "Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength. Worship the LORD in the splendour of his holiness." The heavenly court is summoned to worship before the storm begins.
"The voice of the LORD is over the waters... breaks the cedars... makes Lebanon skip like a calf." Seven thunders. The storm tracks from sea to mountain. God’s voice reshapes geography.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'heavenly beings' — bĕnê 'ēlîm, literally 'sons of gods.' Alter notes the Canaanite background: these are members of the divine council, commanded to prostrate before Yahweh. Monotheism absorbs and subordinates polytheistic imagery.

Alter: 'the voice of the Lord' (qôl YHWH) appears seven times — the number of completeness. The storm moves geographically from the Mediterranean (v.3) through Lebanon (v.5) to the southern desert (v.8). Yahweh's voice spans the entire land.

Calvin: even the angels are commanded to worship. Calvin: if heavenly beings owe God glory, how much more do earthly creatures? The psalm shames human complacency by showing angelic urgency in praise.

Calvin: the cedars of Lebanon — the tallest, strongest trees in the ancient world — shatter like kindling before God's voice. Calvin reads this as a demonstration that no created thing, however magnificent, can withstand divine power.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: bĕnê 'ēlîm ('sons of the mighty') is debated. Options: (1) angelic beings, (2) divine council members, (3) human rulers. The parallel in Ps 89:6 favours heavenly beings in the divine assembly.

NET: 'Sirion' is the Sidonian name for Mount Hermon (cf. Deut 3:9). Using the Phoenician name may deliberately evoke — and subvert — Canaanite storm-god mythology. Yahweh, not Ba'al, controls the thunder.

Kidner: the psalm begins not on earth but in heaven. We overhear the summons to the heavenly court before the storm arrives on earth. Worship begins above and descends — the reverse of human expectation.

Kidner: the sevenfold 'voice of the Lord' creates a sound portrait of a thunderstorm crossing the landscape. Each repetition intensifies — from over the waters, to powerful, to majestic, to cedar-breaking.

VanGemeren: the summons to heavenly beings establishes Yahweh's supremacy over all other claimants to divinity. In the ancient Near East, the storm-god was supreme; Psalm 29 claims that role for Yahweh alone.

VanGemeren: 'the God of glory thunders' — kābôd (glory) and thunder converge. The theophany at Sinai (Exod 19:16-19) is the background: God's voice is not gentle instruction but world-shaking revelation.

Goldingay: many scholars believe Psalm 29 adapts or polemicises against a Canaanite hymn to Ba'al. If so, the psalm's strategy is bold: take the rival's best material and redirect the worship to Yahweh.

Goldingay: 'over the waters' may refer to both the Mediterranean Sea and the primordial waters of chaos. The storm-theophany thus echoes creation — Yahweh's voice that ordered chaos continues to resound.

sections.header: Verses 7–11 — “The LORD Sits Enthroned over the Flood”

heb.paragraph: "The LORD sits enthroned over the flood (mabbūl); the LORD is enthroned as King forever" (v.10). Mabbūl is used elsewhere only for Noah’s flood (Genesis 6–9). God who sent the flood also rules over it. Chaos is not uncontrolled; it is governed.

panels.ctx: More thunders: flashes of lightning (v.7), shakes the desert (v.8), twists oaks and strips forests (v.9). "In his temple all cry, ‘Glory!’" (v.9). Then the throne: God sits enthroned over the flood — the Noah-flood word. The God who unleashed chaos also governs it. "The LORD gives strength to his people; the LORD blesses his people with peace" (v.11). The storm ends in shalom.

cross.note: The mabbūl (flood) — the only other occurrence of this word.
"The seas have lifted up their voice... mightier than the breakers of the sea — the LORD on high is mighty" — the same enthronement-over-waters theology.

notes.note: "In his temple all cry, ‘Glory!’" The storm produces worship. The appropriate response to God’s power is not fear but praise.
"The LORD sits enthroned over the flood. The LORD blesses his people with peace." From storm to peace. From chaos to shalom. The psalm ends where all tempests end: in God’s sovereign calm.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: 'the Lord sits enthroned over the flood' — mabbûl is used elsewhere only of Noah's flood (Gen 6-9). The allusion is deliberate: the God who once judged with flood now reigns over its chaos permanently.
Alter: the psalm's final word is šālôm — peace. After the violence of the storm (shattering, stripping, shaking), the last note is benediction. Raw power serves ultimate tranquillity.

Calvin: God's enthronement over the flood means no chaos — natural or historical — lies outside his sovereignty. Calvin: the same God who rules the tempest rules the turbulence of nations and the storms of the soul.
Calvin: 'strength' and 'peace' are the psalm's final gifts. Calvin notes they come together: peace without strength is vulnerability; strength without peace is tyranny. God gives both.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: mabbûl ('flood') appears only here and in Gen 6-9 in the Hebrew Bible. Its use here is theologically loaded — Yahweh's kingship predates and outlasts the most catastrophic event in biblical memory.

NET: the final petition shifts from description to prayer. After celebrating God's power in nature (vv.3-9) and his eternal reign (v.10), the psalmist asks that this power be channelled to bless his people.
Kidner: 'enthroned over the flood' — the word for flood (mabbûl) appears nowhere else except Genesis 6-9. The deliberate echo says: the God who judged the world then now governs it still, forever.
Kidner: the psalm's final word is peace (šālôm). Seven thunderclaps lead to one benediction. All that terrifying power has a pastoral purpose: to bless his people with peace.

VanGemeren: Yahweh's eternal kingship is the psalm's theological climax. The storm is not an episode but a revelation: the God who speaks in thunder is the God who reigns forever. Power and permanence converge.

VanGemeren: the prayer for 'strength' ('ōz) and 'peace' (šālôm) anticipates the messianic hope — a king who is both mighty warrior and prince of peace (Isa 9:6). The psalm's eschatological horizon is present.

Goldingay: 'enthroned as King forever' — melek lĕ'ôlām. The kingship motif connects to the enthronement psalms (93, 95-99). Yahweh's storm-theophany is his coronation display — not ascending to power but displaying it.
Goldingay: the psalm moves from heaven (v.1) through earth's landscape (vv.3-9) to eternity (v.10) to Israel (v.11). The scope narrows from cosmic to communal: all that power is for 'his people.'

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Then the throne: God sits enthroned over the flood — the Noah-flood word…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe voice of the Lord twists the oaks and strips the forests bare. And in his temple all cry, “Glory!”ESVThe voice of the LORD makes the deer give birth and strips the forests bare, and in his temple all cry, \u201cGlory!\u201dNIVThe voice of the Lord is over the waters; the God of glory thunders, the Lord thunders over the mighty waters.ESVThe voice of the LORD is over the waters; the God of glory thunders, the LORD, over many waters.

rec.text: ""Ascribe to the LORD glory and strength. Worship the LORD in the splendour of his holiness." The heavenly court is summoned to worship before the storm begins."

rows.label: The Throne — 29:7–11
rows.text: Enthroned over THE flood. Storm → "Glory!" → peace. Chaos governed.

hebtext.note: "The voice (qōl) of the LORD is over the waters" (v.3). Seven thunders of the LORD’s voice: over waters (v.3), powerful (v.4), majestic (v.4), breaks cedars (v.5), makes Lebanon skip (v.6). The storm psalm: God’s voice IS the thunder. Each "voice of the LORD" is a thunderclap.

"The LORD sits enthroned over the flood (mabbūl); the LORD is enthroned as King forever" (v.10). Mabbūl is used elsewhere only for Noah’s flood (Genesis 6–9). God who sent the flood also rules over it. Chaos is not uncontrolled; it is governed.

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: seven thunders + enthroned over the flood. Worship at 9: "all cry Glory!" Holiness: the splendour of holiness.
